claims.rationale: McKinnon's 2001-2002 intrusion into ~97 US military and NASA systems via blank/default passwords is well documented in the British and US legal record. The NMCI / NASA / Pentagon counts and his self-described methodology match contemporary reporting.

claims.text: On a US Navy server McKinnon found a spreadsheet titled 'non-terrestrial officers' listing officer names with transfers between ships not in the public US naval registry — which McKinnon interpreted as evidence of a secret human-staffed space fleet.

claims.rationale: McKinnon really did testify (publicly, under oath, in extradition hearings) about a 'non-terrestrial officers' file on a Navy machine. The interpretation that the file proves a secret manned space fleet is McKinnon's inference; the file itself has never been independently produced or verified.

claims.rationale: Donna Hare's testimony about UFO airbrushing is real (she gave it as part of the Disclosure Project, 2001). McKinnon's narrative of seeing the silver cylinder image only exists as his own retelling — there is no screenshot, no second witness, and the image is unrecoverable. The claim is real-as-testimony, unsupported as evidence-of-aliens.

dependencies.rationale: Requires McKinnon's confirmed access to NASA systems.

claims.text: There is real Cold War precedent for secret US space programs running parallel to NASA: the US Air Force's Manned Orbiting Laboratory (MOL) of the 1960s, with separate uniforms, facilities, and secrecy practices, was a planned crewed spy station before President Nixon canceled it in 1969.

its own staff they had uniforms and

claims.rationale: MOL is a real, well-documented USAF program (1963–1969). Cancellation by Nixon administration in June 1969, separate astronaut corps and identifiers, classified spy-platform mission — all confirmed in declassified NRO histories.

claims.rationale: All four facts are documented: 2002 arrest, $800K alleged damages, 70-year theoretical maximum, May's October 2012 decision blocking extradition citing Asperger's and suicide risk. The host states '7 years' rather than 70 in one sentence which is a transcript glitch — the original 70-year framing is correct and used elsewhere in the segment.

claims.text: The 'Solar Warden' mythos describes a multi-vessel secret US (later international) space defense fleet supposedly grown out of Reagan's Strategic Defense Initiative ('Star Wars'), tasked with intercepting unauthorized alien intruders into the solar system.

claims.rationale: This is the standard 'Solar Warden' mythos as it circulates in UFO subcultures. Mainstream defense journalism, declassified records, and budget analysis find no evidence of such a program; the 'descended from SDI' framing is post-hoc storytelling.

claims.text: A June 11, 1985 entry in Reagan's published diaries records the president saying that the US shuttle capacity was such that they 'could orbit 300 people' — a remark that solar-warden believers cite as a slip revealing a much larger covert lift program, since NASA's actual 4-shuttle fleet capped at 32 simultaneous astronauts.

claims.rationale: The Reagan Diaries entry on this date does mention 'we could orbit 300 people'. Conventional readings: Reagan was likely referring to a Space Station Freedom-style multi-launch buildup, or simply confused. The 'covert space program' interpretation is a leap by UFO writers, not the mainstream historical reading.

claims.rationale: The lineage is real: an anonymous 2006 Open Minds Forum post coined the 'Solar Warden' fleet description, and Perks's Nov 2012 Huffington Post (UK Comedy section) piece broadcasted it widely and tied it to McKinnon. Whether the underlying claim is true is a separate matter — see other claims.

dependencies.rationale: Documents the textual origin of the mythos.

claims.text: British amateur astronomer John Lenard Walson's 2007 'luminous layering' technique (combining visible/IR/UV telescope frames) has been used to claim that many apparent stars are actually massive reflective space platforms — the kind of imagery solar-warden believers cite as photographic confirmation.

claims.rationale: Walson's videos are real artifacts, but stacking visible/IR/UV frames from a backyard telescope cannot resolve genuine spaceship structures; the consensus among amateur astronomy reviewers is that the 'platforms' are atmospheric scintillation and image-stacking artifacts of distant stars.

claims.rationale: The Calvine photo case is one of the most-discussed UK MoD UFO files. The host's identification of the Lindsay 2022 release as suspect (B&W vs. color, location mismatch, weather contradiction) matches what mainstream UFO researchers like David Clarke have reported. Witness identities are sealed for 75 years per UK records, which the host slightly misframes as 'classified until 2076'.

claims.rationale: Goode's deposition in his lawsuit against Roger Richards / Light Warrior Productions / Tier Zion did include damaging concessions about the factual basis of his blue-avian narrative. Basiago's Project Pegasus story is a long-running UFO-adjacent claim with no corroboration. Eisenhower's claims are similarly unsupported and her direct relationship to President Eisenhower is the most verifiable thing about her account.

claims.text: Many of the iconic Solar Warden details — moon and Mars bases supplied by carrier ships, ships-named-Sid scanning near-Earth space for alien intruders — match the plot of the 1970 British TV series 'UFO', strongly suggesting the mythos is at least partly cribbed from sci-fi rather than independently sourced.

claims.rationale: Gerry Anderson's 1970 'UFO' series did feature an organization called SHADO with moon bases, sky carriers, and an alien-intruder detection computer (SID). The structural overlap with the Solar Warden description is genuine and well-noted by skeptical UFO researchers like Jason Colavito. This makes a sci-fi origin a plausible explanation for at least the iconography.

dependencies.rationale: Suggests the mythos is fictional in origin.
Whistleblowers may be drawing from the same sci-fi well.

claims.text: The host's verdict: the only piece of the Solar Warden story with any independent grounding is McKinnon's first-person testimony about what he saw, and the operational logistics of running a multi-carrier interplanetary fleet without any rocket-launch detection or supply traffic make a literal Solar Warden essentially impossible to keep secret.

claims.rationale: Standard skeptical position: extraordinary covert programs leak, large-scale launches are tracked globally by all major space-faring states, and decades-long resupply of an interplanetary fleet would be impossible to hide. This is the host's bottom-line and aligns with mainstream defense and space-tracking analysis.

dependencies.rationale: Concludes the mythos is essentially false.
McKinnon's testimony is the strongest residual evidence.
